stem: A PTA is performing iontophoresis using dexamethasone for a patient with lateral epicondylitis. What polarity is used for dexamethasone delivery?
options: Negative electrode — dexamethasone is negatively charged and is placed under the negative (cathode) electrode
rationale: Dexamethasone iontophoresis: negatively charged (placed under cathode/negative electrode). Current drives the drug into tissue via like-charge repulsion. Typical: 40 mA-min dose.

stem: A patient is performing a Nordic hamstring curl. What is the primary benefit of this exercise?
options: Eccentric hamstring strengthening at long muscle lengths — shown to significantly reduce hamstring strain injury risk in athletes
rationale: Nordic hamstring curl: eccentric hamstring strengthening at long muscle lengths. Reduces hamstring strain risk by 50-70% in athletes. Progressive (start with small ROM, increase over time).

stem: A PTA is using fluidotherapy for a patient with hand stiffness. What temperature range is used?
options: 100-118°F (38-48°C) — dry heat delivered through cellulose particles suspended in air, providing heat and desensitization
rationale: Fluidotherapy: 100-118°F, dry heat via cellulose particles. Benefits: heat + desensitization + exercise opportunity (bury/find objects in the medium). Good for hand stiffness and sensitivity.

stem: A patient is performing a clamshell exercise. Which muscle is primarily targeted?
options: Gluteus medius — hip external rotation in side-lying with knees bent targets this hip stabilizer
rationale: Clamshell exercise: side-lying, hips/knees flexed, rotate the top knee open (hip ER). Targets gluteus medius and external rotators. Used for hip abductor strengthening and patellofemoral rehab.

stem: When applying electrical stimulation for muscle re-education, what type of current is typically used?
options: Pulsed biphasic or Russian stimulation — provides motor-level stimulation to facilitate muscle contraction and re-education
rationale: Muscle re-education: pulsed biphasic (NMES) or Russian stimulation. Motor-level intensity producing visible muscle contraction. Combined with volitional effort for best results.

stem: A PTA is performing cervical traction for a patient with cervical radiculopathy. What initial weight is recommended?
options: 10-15 lbs initially (approximately 7-10% of body weight) for mechanical cervical traction, gradually increasing based on response
rationale: Cervical traction: start at 10-15 lbs (intermittent), gradually increase to 25-45 lbs if tolerated. Angle of pull determines which segments are targeted. Contraindicated: instability, RA, malignancy.

stem: A patient is performing a side plank exercise. Which core muscle is primarily working on the bottom side?
options: Internal and external obliques and quadratus lumborum — lateral core stabilizers maintaining the plank position
rationale: Side plank: internal/external obliques and quadratus lumborum (lateral flexion stabilizers). Also gluteus medius (hip abduction stabilization). Excellent lateral core stability exercise.

stem: A patient with a prosthetic leg demonstrates lateral trunk lean toward the prosthetic side during stance. What is the likely cause?
options: Weak hip abductors on the prosthetic side or a prosthesis that is too short
rationale: Lateral trunk lean toward prosthetic side: compensation for weak hip abductors (Trendelenburg) or short prosthesis. Treatment: hip abductor strengthening and/or prosthetic alignment adjustment.

stem: A patient needs a cushioned heel insert for Achilles tendinopathy. What does the heel lift accomplish?
options: Reduces tension on the Achilles tendon by decreasing the ankle dorsiflexion angle during standing and walking
rationale: Heel lift for Achilles tendinopathy: reduces dorsiflexion demand, decreases Achilles tendon tension during stance and gait, and reduces strain on the healing tendon. Typically 6-12mm lift.

stem: A patient with a transtibial amputation is learning to don their suction-suspension prosthesis. What is the correct technique?
options: Pull it on like a boot
Apply lubricant or pull sock over the liner, insert the limb, push out air through the one-way valve, and ensure complete contact without air pockets
rationale: Suction prosthesis donning: apply liner, lubricate or use pull sock, insert limb into socket, expel air through one-way valve (or pull sock through hole), ensure full contact without air pockets.

stem: A patient using a standard wheelchair needs to descend a steep ramp. What is the safest technique?
options: Go backward (facing up the ramp) for better control and to prevent tipping forward out of the wheelchair
rationale: Wheelchair steep ramp descent: go backward (face up the ramp) for optimal control. Prevents forward tipping. Lean back slightly. If with caregiver, the caregiver goes in front (downhill) for safety.

stem: A PTA is performing a Tinetti Balance and Gait Assessment. What does this test evaluate?
options: Balance (sitting, standing, turns) and gait (initiation, step length, symmetry, trunk stability) — with a total score predicting fall risk
rationale: Tinetti (POMA): balance subscale (16 points) + gait subscale (12 points) = 28 total. <19 = high fall risk, 19-24 = moderate fall risk. Assesses multiple balance and gait components.

stem: A geriatric patient with a hip fracture demonstrates fear of falling. What validated tool measures fear of falling?
options: Falls Efficacy Scale (FES) or Activities-specific Balance Confidence (ABC) Scale — self-report questionnaires measuring confidence in performing activities without falling
rationale: Fear of falling measures: FES (10 items, activities of daily living confidence), ABC Scale (16 items, 0-100% confidence). Both predict fall risk and activity avoidance in older adults.

stem: A geriatric patient in a SNF is performing seated exercises due to fall risk. Which seated exercise BEST improves trunk stability?
options: Seated trunk rotation with light resistance, reaching activities that challenge weight shifting, and seated balance perturbations
rationale: Seated trunk stability: trunk rotation with resistance, reaching in multiple directions (weight shifting), perturbation training (gentle pushes), and seated marching (dynamic trunk control).

stem: An elderly patient demonstrates difficulty hearing instructions in a noisy gym. What environmental modification helps?
options: Move to a quieter treatment area, face the patient when speaking, reduce background noise, and use visual demonstration alongside verbal instruction
rationale: Hearing difficulty in gym: quieter area, face the patient (lip reading), reduce background noise, visual demonstration, written backup, confirm understanding, and consider hearing aid check.

stem: At what age does a typically developing child begin to walk independently?
options: 12-15 months — independent walking with a wide base and high guard arm position, gradually normalizing over subsequent months
rationale: Independent walking: 12-15 months (wide base, high guard arms, flat foot). Mature gait pattern develops by 3-4 years (heel-toe, reciprocal arm swing, narrow base).

stem: A child with spastic diplegia (CP) demonstrates scissoring gait. What is the primary muscle group causing this pattern?
options: Hip adductors — spasticity causes excessive adduction and internal rotation of the hips during gait
rationale: Scissoring gait: hip adductor spasticity causes excessive hip adduction (legs cross during walking). Treatment: stretching, botulinum toxin, serial casting, and hip abductor strengthening.

stem: A child with Duchenne muscular dystrophy uses the Gowers' maneuver to stand from the floor. What does this indicate?
options: Proximal muscle weakness — the child walks their hands up their legs to stand because the hip extensors and knee extensors are too weak to rise normally
rationale: Gowers' maneuver: child uses hands on thighs to 'walk up' their own body from the floor. Indicates significant proximal weakness (hip/knee extensors). Classic sign of DMD.

stem: A child with torticollis has which head position?
options: Head tilted to the affected side (tight SCM) and rotated to the opposite side
Head tilted and rotated to the same side
rationale: Torticollis: head tilted toward the tight/affected SCM and rotated away (chin points to the opposite side). Treatment: stretching, positioning, active rotation toward the tight side.

stem: A patient with a posterior hip replacement is told to avoid flexion beyond 90°, adduction past midline, and internal rotation. At which week are these precautions typically discontinued?
options: 6-12 weeks (varies by surgeon) — once the soft tissue has healed sufficiently to prevent posterior dislocation
rationale: Posterior THR precautions: typically 6-12 weeks (surgeon-dependent). Some newer approaches have shorter restriction periods. Always follow the specific surgeon's protocol for discontinuation.

stem: A patient post-ACL reconstruction using a patellar tendon autograft has anterior knee pain. What is a common cause?
options: Patellar tendinopathy or patellofemoral dysfunction at the graft harvest site — common after patellar tendon autograft
rationale: Anterior knee pain after BTB ACL graft: donor site morbidity — patellar tendinopathy, patellofemoral pain, and kneeling pain. Treatment: patellar mobilization, quad strengthening, and gradual loading.

stem: A patient with a humeral shaft fracture treated with ORIF is at 8 weeks. What exercise is appropriate?
options: Active ROM of the shoulder and elbow, gentle progressive resistance as tolerated, and functional activities within the surgeon's weight-bearing restrictions
rationale: Humeral shaft ORIF at 8 weeks: AROM shoulder/elbow, gentle progressive resistance, functional activities, and follow surgeon weight restrictions. Monitor fracture healing with follow-up imaging.

stem: A patient with de Quervain's tenosynovitis has a positive Finkelstein's test. What treatment does the PTA provide?
options: Thumb spica splint, modality application (US, ice), tendon gliding exercises, activity modification, and gentle stretching after acute phase
rationale: De Quervain's PT: thumb spica splint (rest), modalities (US, ice for inflammation), tendon gliding exercises, activity modification (avoid repetitive thumb use), and gradual stretching.

stem: A patient with a medial meniscus tear (non-surgical management) is at 4 weeks. What exercise progression is appropriate?
options: Progressive ROM, quadriceps and hamstring strengthening (avoid deep flexion), stationary bike, and proprioceptive training
rationale: Medial meniscus conservative at 4 weeks: progressive ROM (avoid deep flexion >90° initially), quad/hamstring strengthening, bike, proprioception, and avoid pivoting/twisting activities.

stem: A patient recovering from a distal humerus fracture has limited elbow flexion (90°) at 10 weeks. What intervention may help?
options: Low-load prolonged stretch, dynamic or static progressive splinting, joint mobilization (posterior glides for flexion), and sustained stretching
rationale: Elbow flexion limitation at 10 weeks: low-load prolonged stretch, dynamic/static progressive splinting, joint mobilization (posterior glides), heat before stretching, and patient compliance with HEP.

stem: A patient with chronic ankle instability fails the Star Excursion Balance Test (SEBT). What does this test evaluate?
options: Dynamic balance and neuromuscular control — reaching in multiple directions while maintaining single-leg stance
rationale: SEBT/Y-Balance: dynamic balance and neuromuscular control. Single-leg stance while reaching in anterior, posteromedial, and posterolateral directions. Asymmetry predicts injury risk.

stem: A PTA is documenting a patient's wound using the PUSH tool. What parameters are scored?
options: Wound surface area, exudate amount, and wound surface type (tissue type) — the three components of the Pressure Ulcer Scale for Healing
rationale: PUSH tool (Pressure Ulcer Scale for Healing): surface area (0-10), exudate amount (0-3: none to heavy), surface type (0-4: closed to necrotic). Total score tracks healing over time.

stem: When documenting a patient's functional progress, which type of goal is MOST defensible for justifying continued PT services?
options: Functional goals — directly related to meaningful activities the patient needs to perform (e.g., 'walk 300 ft to the mailbox independently')
rationale: Functional goals are most defensible: relate to meaningful activities (walking to mailbox, climbing stairs at home, returning to work). Connect impairment improvements to real-world function.

stem: A PTA documents: 'Pt reports pain 7/10 in low back radiating to left posterior thigh, worse with prolonged sitting, better with walking.' Which SOAP section is this?
options: Subjective — the patient's self-report of symptoms, pain, and what makes it better or worse
rationale: Subjective section: patient's self-report — pain description, location, intensity, aggravating/relieving factors, functional complaints, and any other patient-reported information.

stem: A patient with C. difficile infection requires PT. What type of precautions are needed?
options: Contact precautions with soap and water hand hygiene (alcohol-based rubs are NOT effective against C. diff spores) and enhanced environmental cleaning with bleach
rationale: C. difficile: contact precautions, SOAP AND WATER hand hygiene (alcohol-based sanitizers do not kill C. diff spores), and bleach-based environmental cleaning. Gown and gloves required.

stem: A wound is measured and documented by clock face orientation. Undermining at '12 o'clock to 3 o'clock' refers to which direction?
options: Superior to the patient's right — the 12 o'clock position is always toward the head (cephalad) regardless of wound location
rationale: Wound clock orientation: 12 o'clock = toward the head (cephalad), 6 = toward the feet, 3 = patient's right, 9 = patient's left. Standardized regardless of wound location on the body.

stem: A patient with a wound is receiving pulsed lavage. What pressure range is appropriate for wound irrigation?
options: 4-15 psi — sufficient to remove debris and bacteria without damaging healthy granulation tissue
rationale: Pulsed lavage: 4-15 psi for wound irrigation. Below 4 psi is insufficient for debridement. Above 15 psi may damage granulation tissue and drive bacteria deeper into the wound.

stem: A patient's blood pressure drops 25 mmHg systolic when moving from supine to standing. What is this called?
options: Orthostatic hypotension — a drop of ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic within 3 minutes of standing
rationale: Orthostatic hypotension: SBP drop ≥20 mmHg or DBP drop ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing. Common with medications, dehydration, deconditioning, and autonomic dysfunction.

stem: A patient on oxygen therapy via nasal cannula at 2 L/min is participating in PT. What approximate FiO2 does this provide?
options: 28% — each liter of nasal cannula flow adds approximately 4% to the room air FiO2 of 21%
rationale: Nasal cannula FiO2: approximately 21% + (4% × L/min). At 2 L/min: ~28% FiO2. At 4 L/min: ~36%. Maximum 6 L/min (~44%). Higher flows require high-flow nasal cannula or mask.

stem: A PTA notices a patient's surgical incision has increased redness, warmth, and purulent drainage. What action is required?
options: Stop treatment, notify the PT and physician immediately — these are signs of wound infection requiring medical evaluation
Document only at end of day
rationale: Wound infection signs (redness, warmth, purulent drainage, possible fever): stop treatment, notify PT/physician immediately, document findings, and do not apply any treatments to the wound.

stem: A PTA is treating a minor patient (under 18). Which ethical principle requires obtaining parental consent before treatment?
options: Autonomy — minors cannot provide legal consent; parental/guardian informed consent is required before initiating treatment
rationale: Minor consent: parental/guardian informed consent required (autonomy principle for minors). The minor should still receive age-appropriate information and assent. Emancipated minors can self-consent.

stem: A PTA is asked to provide PT services to a family member. What is the ethical concern?
options: No concern — family treatment is fine
Dual relationship — treating family members creates a conflict between professional objectivity and personal relationship, potentially compromising care quality
Only if it's a distant relative
Only if no other therapists are available
rationale: Treating family: dual relationship concerns — compromised objectivity, difficulty maintaining professional boundaries, and potential for biased clinical decision-making. Best practice: refer to another provider.

stem: A PTA witnesses another PTA performing an intervention outside their scope of practice. What is the appropriate action?
options: Report the concern to the supervising PT and, if necessary, the facility compliance officer — patient safety is the priority
rationale: Scope violation: report to the supervising PT immediately (patient safety priority). If not resolved, escalate to facility compliance, state licensing board, or APTA ethics commission.

stem: When a PTA identifies a patient need that requires a service outside of PT (e.g., nutritional counseling), the PTA should:
options: Communicate the identified need to the supervising PT for appropriate referral to the relevant discipline
Ask the patient's family to handle it
rationale: Referral for non-PT needs: communicate the identified need to the PT, who can make the appropriate referral. The PTA identifies the need but does not refer independently (follows PT direction).

stem: A PTA is communicating with a patient who speaks limited English. What is the MOST appropriate communication strategy?
options: Use a qualified medical interpreter (not family members for clinical information), visual demonstrations, and simple/clear language with confirmation of understanding
Use Google Translate for all communication
rationale: Limited English: qualified medical interpreter (not family — accuracy, confidentiality), visual demonstrations, simple language, teach-back for understanding, and translated written materials.

stem: A patient with a cultural belief system that conflicts with the prescribed treatment approach. How should the PTA handle this?
options: Respect the patient's cultural beliefs, discuss alternatives with the PT that align with both the treatment goals and the patient's values, and seek cultural consultation if needed
Force compliance with the standard approach
rationale: Cultural sensitivity: respect beliefs, discuss alternatives with the PT, find culturally acceptable approaches, seek cultural consultation, and document the adapted plan of care.

stem: A patient with a stroke has Broca's aphasia. What communication approach is MOST effective?
options: Ask yes/no questions, allow ample response time, use gestures and communication boards — the patient understands but has difficulty producing speech
rationale: Broca's (expressive) aphasia: comprehension relatively intact but speech production impaired. Yes/no questions, allow time, don't finish sentences, use gestures/boards, and acknowledge frustration.

stem: A PTA is working with the nursing staff to establish a consistent turning schedule for a patient at risk for pressure injuries. What is the recommended interval?
options: Every 2 hours (or more frequently based on individual risk assessment and skin tolerance)
rationale: Pressure injury prevention turning: every 2 hours minimum (30° side to side), more frequently based on risk level, skin tolerance, and support surface type. Individualize based on assessment.

stem: A patient post-cardiac surgery is in the ICU on day 2. What PT intervention is appropriate?
options: Bed mobility, seated edge of bed (if hemodynamically stable), deep breathing, ankle pumps, and gentle AROM within sternal precautions
rationale: Post-cardiac surgery ICU Day 2: bed mobility, sitting edge of bed (monitor vitals), deep breathing/incentive spirometry, ankle pumps, gentle AROM, and maintain sternal precautions.

stem: A patient with Parkinson's disease demonstrates festinating gait (involuntary acceleration). What cue helps control this?
options: External rhythmic cueing (metronome, counting, music beat) and visual cues (lines on the floor) to regulate step timing and length
rationale: Festinating gait control: external auditory cues (metronome, counting), visual cues (floor lines, laser), conscious attention to step length, walking to music with a set tempo.

stem: A patient recovering from Guillain-Barré syndrome is beginning to show motor recovery. What is the typical recovery pattern?
options: Proximal to distal (recovery begins in the proximal muscles and progresses distally — opposite of the ascending weakness pattern)
rationale: GBS recovery pattern: proximal to distal (opposite of the ascending paralysis). Recovery begins with proximal muscles (shoulders, hips) and progresses to distal (hands, feet). Can take months to years.

stem: A patient with type 1 diabetes is exercising and suddenly becomes pale, sweaty, and confused. What is happening and what should the PTA do?
options: Hypoglycemia — stop exercise, give fast-acting sugar (juice, glucose tabs, candy), monitor response, and notify medical staff
rationale: Hypoglycemia signs: pallor, sweating, confusion, shakiness, rapid heartbeat. Stop exercise, give 15g fast-acting carbohydrate, recheck in 15 minutes, and notify medical staff. Do not resume exercise.

stem: A patient with a compression fracture at T12 is being treated conservatively with a TLSO. What PT goals are appropriate?
options: Pain management, postural education (avoid flexion), gentle extension exercises, core isometrics, and fall prevention
rationale: T12 compression fracture conservative PT: pain management, avoid trunk flexion (worsens fracture), gentle extension exercises, core stabilization, posture education, and fall prevention.

stem: A patient with a chronic wound that has been present for over 30 days is not healing. What factors should the PTA assess?
options: Nutritional status, blood glucose control (diabetics), infection, blood supply (vascular status), pressure relief adequacy, and compliance with the treatment plan
rationale: Non-healing chronic wound assessment: nutrition (albumin, pre-albumin), glucose control, infection (culture if suspected), vascular status (ABI), pressure relief, compliance, and medications affecting healing.

stem: A patient on bed rest for 3 weeks is starting PT. What physiological changes has prolonged bed rest caused?
options: Deconditioning including decreased cardiovascular fitness, muscle atrophy, bone loss, orthostatic intolerance, and increased risk of DVT/PE
rationale: Prolonged bed rest effects: cardiovascular deconditioning (3% VO2max/day), muscle atrophy, bone loss, orthostatic intolerance, DVT risk, skin breakdown risk, and psychological effects.

stem: A patient needs a progressive return to work plan after a lumbar fusion. What elements should the plan include?
options: Graduated work hours, modified duties, ergonomic workstation setup, lifting restrictions per surgeon, and periodic reassessment of work capacity
No return to work for 1 year
rationale: Return to work after fusion: graduated hours (part-time → full-time), modified duties, ergonomic setup, lifting restrictions (surgeon-specified), body mechanics training, and reassessment timeline.

stem: A PTA is developing a plyometric training program for a post-ACL reconstruction patient at 6 months. What is the correct progression?
options: Double-leg jumps → single-leg hops → lateral hops → sport-specific jumping — progress only when each level is pain-free and well-controlled
rationale: ACL plyometric progression: bilateral jumping → single-leg hopping → lateral/diagonal movements → sport-specific activities. Each level must be pain-free, well-controlled, and meet strength criteria.

stem: When educating a patient about the 'RICE' principle for acute injury management, what does each letter stand for?
options: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation — the classic first-aid approach for acute musculoskeletal injuries to manage pain and swelling
rationale: RICE: Rest (protect from further injury), Ice (20 min on/off, reduce inflammation), Compression (elastic wrap, reduce swelling), Elevation (above heart level, reduce edema).

stem: When educating a patient about proper backpack use to prevent back pain, what guidelines should the PTA provide?
options: Use both straps, keep the pack close to the body, limit weight to 10-15% of body weight, and use the waist strap for heavier loads
Fill it as heavy as possible
rationale: Backpack guidelines: both straps (distribute weight), 10-15% of body weight maximum, close to body, waist strap for heavy loads, heaviest items closest to the back, and adjust to mid-back level.

stem: A patient asks about the correct technique for getting in and out of bed to protect their back. What technique should the PTA teach?
options: Sit up with a crunch motion
Log roll to the side, push up with arms while swinging legs over the edge — maintaining neutral spine throughout the movement
Twist and reach for the bedside table
rationale: Bed mobility for back protection: log roll (maintain spinal alignment), push up with arms while swinging legs off the edge, reverse for lying down. Avoid trunk flexion and twisting.

stem: When educating a patient about the use of ice for acute injury, what is the recommended application time?
options: 15-20 minutes per application with a barrier between ice and skin, every 1-2 hours for the first 48-72 hours
rationale: Ice application: 15-20 minutes per session (through the 4 stages: cold → burning → aching → numbness), barrier between ice/skin, every 1-2 hours, for 48-72 hours post-injury.

stem: A PTA is working with a speech-language pathologist (SLP) regarding a patient with dysphagia and aspiration risk. What information does the PTA provide?
options: The patient's positioning during activities, head control, trunk stability, and alertness level — factors that affect safe swallowing during PT sessions
rationale: PTA-SLP collaboration for dysphagia: positioning during PT, head/trunk control, alertness level, fatigue during therapy, and any coughing/choking observed during PT activities.

stem: A patient recovering from a traumatic brain injury needs to return to driving. What professional should perform the evaluation?
options: A Certified Driver Rehabilitation Specialist (CDRS) — the PTA can report relevant physical and cognitive observations to support the referral
rationale: TBI driving evaluation: CDRS performs comprehensive evaluation (cognitive, visual, physical, behind-the-wheel). PTA provides relevant information (balance, reaction time, cognitive observations) to support referral.

stem: A PTA is coordinating with the orthotist about a patient's new AFO. What clinical feedback is important?
options: Gait deviations with/without the AFO, skin condition, patient comfort, functional performance changes, and alignment observations during gait
rationale: PTA-orthotist collaboration: gait analysis with/without AFO, skin condition (pressure areas), comfort reports, functional improvements, and alignment observations for adjustment recommendations.

stem: A patient with multiple sclerosis is experiencing an exacerbation with increased weakness and fatigue. How should the PTA modify the exercise program?
options: Significantly reduce exercise intensity, focus on gentle ROM and functional mobility, allow frequent rest breaks, and avoid overheating
Increase intensity to fight the weakness
rationale: MS exacerbation: significantly reduce intensity, gentle ROM and functional mobility, frequent rest breaks, avoid overheating (Uhthoff's), and progress cautiously as symptoms improve.

stem: A patient with a below-knee amputation is ready for initial prosthetic training. What is the first step?
options: Donning and doffing the prosthesis correctly — the patient must master applying and removing the prosthesis before any walking training
rationale: Initial prosthetic training sequence: donning/doffing → standing balance → weight shifting → stepping → parallel bar walking → progressive ambulation with appropriate assistive device.

stem: A geriatric patient with a fear of falling avoids going outside. What approach addresses both the physical and psychological aspects?
options: Cognitive-behavioral approach combined with graded physical challenges: address fear beliefs, build confidence through progressive achievable tasks, and establish social support
rationale: Fear of falling: combined approach — exercise (balance, strength), cognitive-behavioral (address catastrophic thinking), graded exposure (progressive challenges), and social support/group exercise.

stem: A patient with an ankle sprain (Grade 2) is at 3 weeks. What functional test indicates readiness for return to sport?
options: Single-leg hop test >90% of uninvolved side, full ROM, adequate strength (5/5), functional agility tests, and sport-specific activities without pain or instability
rationale: Ankle sprain return-to-sport criteria: single-leg hop >90% of uninvolved, full ROM, strength 5/5, agility tests (T-test, shuttle run), sport-specific activities, and proprioceptive control.

stem: A patient asks about the benefits of Nordic walking (walking with poles). What can the PTA share?
options: Increased upper body engagement, higher caloric expenditure (20-46% more than regular walking), improved balance and stability, and reduced lower extremity joint loading
rationale: Nordic walking benefits: increased upper body engagement, 20-46% more caloric expenditure, improved balance (4 points of contact), reduced LE joint loading, and improved cardiovascular fitness.

stem: A PTA is treating a patient with a new tracheostomy. The patient desaturates to 86% during mobilization. What action should the PTA take?
options: Stop activity immediately, return the patient to rest position, ensure oxygen supply is connected, suction if indicated, and notify nursing/respiratory therapy
rationale: Tracheostomy desaturation: stop activity, rest position, verify O2 supply, suction if secretions are the cause, monitor SpO2 recovery, notify nursing/respiratory, and document the event.

stem: A patient is performing a standing calf raise exercise. During the lowering phase (heel drops below neutral), which type of contraction is occurring?
options: Eccentric contraction of the gastrocnemius and soleus — controlling the lowering (lengthening) phase against gravity
rationale: Calf raise lowering phase: eccentric contraction of the gastrocnemius/soleus (plantar flexors lengthen while controlling the descent). The raising phase is concentric plantar flexion.

stem: A patient with a frozen shoulder is in the 'thawing' phase. What characterizes this phase?
options: Gradual return of ROM with decreasing pain — the capsule is slowly loosening and mobility is improving
rationale: Thawing phase (adhesive capsulitis): gradual ROM improvement, decreasing pain, can be aggressive with stretching/mobilization, and full recovery may take 12-24 months from onset.

stem: A PTA is assessing a patient's respiratory status before exercise. What findings would indicate the patient is NOT safe to exercise?
options: Resting SpO2 <88%, respiratory rate >30/min, accessory muscle use at rest, and significant dyspnea at rest — all indicate respiratory distress
rationale: Respiratory exercise contraindications: SpO2 <88% at rest, RR >30/min, significant accessory muscle use at rest, severe resting dyspnea, and hemodynamic instability.

stem: A patient recovering from a spinal cord injury wants to participate in wheelchair basketball. What is the PTA's role?
options: Support adaptive sport participation through sport-specific training, wheelchair skills, strengthening for sport demands, and referral to adaptive sports programs
rationale: Adaptive sport support: sport-specific training, advanced wheelchair skills, sport conditioning, injury prevention, referral to adaptive sports programs, and advocate for participation in meaningful activities.

stem: A patient with chronic low back pain has been using passive modalities (heat, ultrasound) for 8 weeks with minimal improvement. What change in approach is needed?
options: Transition to active interventions: exercise-based treatment (core stabilization, flexibility, aerobic), pain neuroscience education, and graded activity program
rationale: Chronic LBP evidence: active interventions (exercise, education) are superior to passive modalities for chronic pain. Transition to movement-based treatment, self-management strategies, and graded activity.

stem: A child with spina bifida at L3 level has which expected functional mobility?
options: Household ambulation with bilateral KAFOs and forearm crutches — may use wheelchair for community distances
rationale: L3 spina bifida: quad function present but hip abductors/extensors weak. Household ambulation with bilateral KAFOs and crutches. Wheelchair often preferred for community mobility.

stem: A patient on mechanical ventilation in the ICU is receiving early mobilization PT. What is the primary goal?
options: Prevent deconditioning, maintain ROM and muscle function, reduce ICU-acquired weakness, and promote weaning from mechanical ventilation
rationale: ICU early mobilization: prevent deconditioning, reduce ICU-acquired weakness, maintain ROM, facilitate ventilator weaning, reduce delirium, and improve functional outcomes at discharge.

stem: When applying ultrasound over a metal implant (e.g., plate and screws), what is the current evidence-based recommendation?
options: Use caution — reduce intensity, use pulsed mode, and avoid if the metal is superficial; current evidence suggests deep metal implants may tolerate therapeutic US
rationale: US over metal implants: use caution, not absolute contraindication for deep implants. Reduce intensity, pulsed mode, monitor closely. Avoid if metal is superficial (<1 cm). Evidence evolving.

stem: A patient is performing a lateral band walk (monster walk). What muscle is primarily targeted?
options: Gluteus medius and minimus — lateral stepping against band resistance strengthens the hip abductors in a functional pattern
rationale: Lateral band walk: targets gluteus medius/minimus (hip abductors) in a functional pattern. Used for patellofemoral rehab, hip strengthening, and correcting dynamic valgus during functional activities.

stem: A patient recovering from a meniscal repair (not meniscectomy) has which additional restriction compared to meniscectomy?
options: Limited weight-bearing and restricted deep knee flexion (typically >90°) for 4-6 weeks to protect the repair and allow healing
rationale: Meniscal repair vs. meniscectomy: repair requires limited WB (TTWB-PWB), restricted flexion (>90° avoided), and slower progression (4-6 weeks for WB, 4-6 months for full activity vs. 4-6 weeks for meniscectomy).

stem: A patient with a posterior tibialis tendon dysfunction (Stage II) demonstrates pes planus and difficulty performing a single heel rise. What intervention is appropriate?
options: Custom orthotic with medial arch support, posterior tibialis strengthening, ankle stabilization exercises, and potential use of an Arizona brace for more support
rationale: PTTD Stage II treatment: custom orthotic (medial arch support), posterior tibialis strengthening, ankle stabilization, AFO/Arizona brace (if needed), and eccentric exercises.

stem: A PTA documenting a home visit notes multiple fall hazards. How should this be documented?
options: List specific hazards identified (loose rugs, poor lighting, cord placement), recommendations made, patient/caregiver response, and follow-up plan
rationale: Home hazard documentation: specific hazards identified (location, type), recommendations provided, patient/caregiver response, modifications made during visit, and follow-up plan.

stem: A patient's wound has 100% yellow slough covering the wound bed. Can the wound be staged?
options: No — a wound with 100% slough covering the wound bed cannot be accurately staged and is classified as 'unstageable' until the slough is removed
rationale: Unstageable wound: slough or eschar covering the wound bed prevents accurate depth assessment and staging. Must debride before staging (unless stable eschar on heel — do not remove).

stem: A patient with peripheral vascular disease has an ankle-brachial index (ABI) of 0.5. What does this indicate?
options: Severe peripheral arterial disease — ABI <0.5 indicates severe ischemia with increased risk of tissue loss and may require surgical intervention
rationale: ABI interpretation: >1.0 = normal, 0.8-1.0 = mild PAD, 0.5-0.8 = moderate PAD, <0.5 = severe PAD (claudication at rest, tissue loss risk). <0.5 may need surgical intervention.

stem: A patient post-stroke is at Brunnstrom Stage 4 of motor recovery. What does this indicate?
options: Movement outside of synergy patterns begins to emerge — the patient can perform some isolated joint movements separate from the full synergy
rationale: Brunnstrom Stage 4: movement outside of synergy begins. Some isolated joint movement (e.g., hand to sacrum, knee flexion in standing). Transition from synergy-bound to selective movement.

stem: A patient with a vestibular hypofunction is performing gaze stabilization exercises. What is the exercise?
options: Fix the eyes on a stationary target while moving the head horizontally and vertically (VOR × 1 exercise) to improve the vestibulo-ocular reflex
Only eye exercises without head movement
rationale: Gaze stabilization (VOR × 1): fix eyes on a stationary target, move head horizontally/vertically while maintaining focus. Promotes vestibular compensation and improves VOR gain.

stem: A PTA notices that a patient's walker tennis balls are worn through. What should the PTA do?
options: Replace the tennis balls (or recommend walker glides) to maintain proper walker function, floor contact, and prevent falls from catching/sticking
Add tape over the worn area
rationale: Walker maintenance: replace worn tennis balls/glides (prevent catching/tripping), check rubber tips on walker legs, ensure no loose parts, and verify height adjustment is correct.

stem: A geriatric patient is prescribed a home exercise program but has no equipment at home. What exercises require no equipment?
options: Chair squats, wall push-ups, standing balance exercises, walking program, stair climbing (if available), and heel/toe raises using body weight
rationale: No-equipment HEP: chair squats, wall push-ups, single-leg stance, tandem walking, heel/toe raises, stair climbing, walking program, and seated exercises for the home-bound patient.

stem: When educating a post-surgical patient about incision care, what signs of infection should they report?
options: Increasing redness spreading from the incision, warmth, swelling, purulent drainage (yellow/green), fever >101°F, and increasing pain at the incision site
rationale: Surgical wound infection signs: spreading redness (beyond normal healing redness), increased warmth, swelling, purulent drainage, fever >101°F (38.3°C), increasing pain, and wound dehiscence.

stem: A PTA is treating a child with torticollis. What is the primary stretching direction?
options: Lateral flexion AWAY from the tight side and rotation TOWARD the tight side — stretching the shortened sternocleidomastoid muscle
rationale: Torticollis stretching: lateral flexion away from the tight SCM (opposite side) and rotation toward the tight side (look toward tight SCM). Gentle, sustained stretches during diaper changes and play.

stem: A patient with a cervical spinal cord injury at C5 is working on wheelchair propulsion. What adaptation allows independent propulsion?
options: Projection (peg) rims or coated wheel rims — the patient lacks grip but can use the palm/wrist extensors to push against the adapted rims
rationale: C5 SCI wheelchair: projection (peg) rims or friction-coated rims allow propulsion using palm pressure and wrist extension (no grip). Propulsion is functional but less efficient than lower-level injuries.

stem: A patient is performing a double-leg bridge with a ball squeeze between the knees. What additional muscle is recruited compared to a standard bridge?
options: Hip adductors (adductor magnus, longus, brevis) — the ball squeeze adds adductor activation to the bridge exercise
rationale: Bridge with ball squeeze: adds hip adductor activation (isometric contraction to squeeze) in addition to standard bridge muscles (glutes, hamstrings, core). Used for adductor and pelvic floor activation.

stem: A patient asks the PTA for advice about a supplement they saw advertised for joint health. What is the PTA's appropriate response?
options: Explain that specific supplement recommendations are outside the PTA's scope; suggest discussing with their physician or a registered dietitian
rationale: Supplement advice: outside PTA scope. Refer to physician or registered dietitian. The PTA can provide general education about nutrition principles related to exercise and recovery.

stem: A PTA is treating a patient with a total shoulder replacement who asks about returning to golf. What guidance is appropriate?
options: Discuss with the surgeon regarding timeline (typically 4-6 months), start with putting and chipping before full swing, and use proper mechanics training
rationale: TSA return to golf: surgeon clearance (typically 4-6 months), progressive return (putting → chipping → short irons → full swing), proper mechanics, adequate strength and ROM prerequisites.

stem: A PTA is assessing a patient's dynamic balance using the Dynamic Gait Index (DGI). What activities are tested?
options: Walking at different speeds, head turns during walking, stepping over obstacles, pivoting turns, and stair climbing — assessing balance during functional gait tasks
rationale: DGI: 8 items — gait on level surface, speed changes, horizontal/vertical head turns, stepping over obstacles, pivoting turns, and stair climbing. Score ≤19/24 = fall risk.

stem: A patient with a total hip replacement has been told they can discontinue precautions. What functional test should the PTA perform before advanced activities?
options: Hip abductor strength testing, Trendelenburg test, single-leg stance, and functional mobility assessment to ensure adequate strength and stability before progressing activities
rationale: Post-precaution THR testing: hip abductor strength (Trendelenburg), single-leg stance, functional mobility (stairs, TUG), gait analysis, and pain assessment before advancing activities.

stem: A PTA is performing manual lymph drainage (MLD) for a patient with upper extremity lymphedema. What direction should the strokes go?
options: Proximal to distal clearance first (clear the trunk/proximal areas), then distal to proximal strokes on the limb to move fluid toward cleared areas
rationale: MLD technique: clear proximal areas first (trunk, axilla), then work distally to proximally on the limb. Light pressure (1.5 oz), slow rhythmic strokes, and follow lymphatic pathways.

stem: A patient undergoing chemotherapy asks about exercising during treatment. What general guidance can the PTA provide?
options: Light-to-moderate exercise is generally safe and beneficial during chemo (reduces fatigue, improves mood, maintains function) — adjust based on blood counts and symptoms each session
rationale: Exercise during chemotherapy: generally safe and beneficial (reduces fatigue, maintains function). Adjust based on blood counts, symptoms, and energy level each session. Avoid contact/fall risk if platelets low.

stem: A patient with a new stroke has flaccidity in the affected upper extremity. What is the primary concern?
options: Shoulder subluxation due to lack of muscular support — position the arm to prevent subluxation and educate all caregivers on proper handling
rationale: Flaccid UE post-stroke: shoulder subluxation risk (no muscular support). Proper positioning (arm supported), caregiver education (no pulling), arm trough in wheelchair, and consider sling for ambulation.

stem: A patient with a cervical spinal cord injury is performing a pressure relief in their wheelchair. How long should they hold the weight shift?
options: 15-30 seconds minimum — sufficient time to allow tissue reperfusion and prevent pressure injury development
rationale: Wheelchair pressure relief: 15-30 seconds minimum, every 15-30 minutes. Methods: push-up (if able), lean forward, lean sideways, or tilt/recline. Duration must allow tissue reperfusion.

stem: A PTA is using biofeedback to help a patient improve quadriceps activation after TKR. What type of biofeedback is used?
options: EMG biofeedback — surface electrodes over the VMO/quads provide visual and auditory feedback of muscle activation to improve volitional contraction
rationale: EMG biofeedback for quad activation: surface electrodes on the VMO/quads display muscle electrical activity. Patient sees/hears contraction intensity. Facilitates re-education post-surgically.

stem: A patient recovering from a TKR demonstrates a flexion contracture of 10° at 6 weeks. What intervention is MOST appropriate?
options: Prone hang (passive extension), heel prop exercises, low-load prolonged stretch, and cryotherapy-assisted stretching to achieve full extension
rationale: TKR flexion contracture: prone hang, heel prop (towel under ankle, let gravity extend), low-load prolonged stretch, quadriceps sets in full extension, and cryotherapy before stretching.

stem: A geriatric patient with a history of falls is participating in a group exercise program. What type of exercise class has the BEST evidence for fall reduction?
options: Tai Chi or Otago Exercise Program — both have strong evidence for reducing fall incidence in community-dwelling older adults
rationale: Best fall prevention exercise evidence: Tai Chi (reduces falls 20-40%) and Otago Exercise Program (strength + balance, reduces falls 35%). Both are structured, progressive, and community-implementable.

stem: A patient with bilateral knee OA asks about the benefits of aquatic therapy versus land-based exercise. What can the PTA explain?
options: Aquatic therapy provides similar strength and aerobic benefits with less joint loading (buoyancy) and reduced pain during exercise; ideal for patients who cannot tolerate land-based exercise
rationale: Aquatic vs. land OA: similar outcomes for strength and function. Aquatic advantages: reduced joint loading, less pain during exercise, and warmth aids relaxation. Good alternative when land exercise is too painful.

stem: A patient asks the PTA why they need to continue PT exercises at home after being discharged. What is the MOST important reason?
options: To maintain and continue the functional gains achieved in therapy — without ongoing exercise, the benefits will diminish and symptoms may return
rationale: HEP importance: maintain gains (detraining principle), continue progress, prevent recurrence, promote self-management, long-term health benefits, and reduce need for future PT episodes.

stem: A patient with a new colostomy is concerned about exercising. What guidance can the PTA provide?
options: Most exercises are safe with a colostomy — avoid heavy lifting initially (hernia risk), wear an abdominal support belt during activity, and empty the pouch before exercise
rationale: Colostomy exercise: most activities safe. Avoid heavy lifting initially (hernia risk at stoma site), wear support belt, empty pouch pre-exercise, stay hydrated, and gradually return to previous activities.
